items.title: Linear models and neural network layers
items.description: In linear regression, logistic regression and dense neural network layers, predictions are often computed as Y = XW + b, where X is a batch of input features and W is a weight matrix. Matrix multiplication applies the same linear transformation to every row in the batch in one operation.

items.title: Geometric transforms in graphics and robotics
items.description: 2D and 3D rotations, scalings, shears and translations in homogeneous coordinates are all represented by matrices. Combining multiple camera, world or robot-frame transforms becomes a product of matrices that can be precomputed or applied in sequence.

items.title: Encoding and solving systems of linear equations
items.description: A system of linear equations Ax = b is represented with a coefficient matrix A. Algorithms such as Gaussian elimination and matrix factorisations manipulate A using matrix multiplication-like operations to find x or study properties of the system.

items.title: Graph algorithms and Markov chains
items.description: Powers of an adjacency matrix count paths of different lengths in a graph, and powers of a stochastic transition matrix propagate probability distributions in Markov chains. These operations all rely on matrix multiplication.
